bibliographic_data.title: Towards Provable Security in Industrial Control Systems Via Dynamic Protocol Attestation
bibliographic_data.abstract: Industrial control systems (ICSs) increasingly rely on digital technologies vulnerable to cyber attacks. Cyber attackers can infiltrate ICSs and execute malicious actions, causing the system to enter an unsafe state. This paper introduces a methodology that restricts actions using protocols, ensuring only safe actions are executed. Protocols are written in a domain-specific language embedded in an interactive theorem prover (ITP) to enable formal, machine-checked proofs of safety properties. Dynamic attestation ensures ICSs conform to their protocol even if an adversary compromises a component. The approach is demonstrated with an example from the Fischertechnik Industry 4.0 platform and measures the impact on latency and throughput.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a new methodology for developing ICS software that uses formal methods to ensure safety properties are maintained through dynamic protocol attestation.
core_content.key_methodology: Protocols are written in a domain-specific language embedded in an interactive theorem prover (ITP) to enable formal, machine-checked proofs of safety properties. Dynamic attestation ensures ICSs conform to their protocols even if an adversary compromises a component.
outcomes: A methodology for developing ICS software that uses formal methods to ensure safety properties are maintained through dynamic protocol attestation.
Automation to extract RPC framework inputs from F* specifications, generating the code necessary to create protocol implementations.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Industrial control systems (ICSs) increasingly rely on digital technologies vulnerable to cyber attacks. Cyber attackers can infiltrate ICSs and execute malicious actions, causing the system to enter an unsafe state. This paper introduces a methodology that restricts actions using protocols, ensuring only safe actions are executed. Protocols are written in a domain-specific language embedded in an interactive theorem prover (ITP) to enable formal, machine-checked proofs of safety properties. Dynamic attestation ensures ICSs conform to their protocol even if an adversary compromises a component. The approach is demonstrated with an example from the Fischertechnik Industry 4.0 platform and measures the impact on latency and throughput.

The main idea is to restrict sequences of RPCs to a protocol, proving that protocol conformance implies the ICS behaves safely. Unsafe behavior can only occur as a result of executing non-conformant RPCs. Non-conformant RPCs are prevented using a dynamic protocol attestation system. When the attestation system encounters a non-conformant RPC, it triggers a fail-safe, which is always safe to execute.

Engineers describe their system’s RPCs using an interactive theorem prover (ITP). Inside F*, a state-of-the-art ITP with sophisticated proof automation, a domain-specific language (DSL) helps engineers design RPC protocols. Engineers use F*’s proof automation to prove that protocol conformance implies safety.

The approach provides automation to generate code for RPC frameworks from the DSL. Although ITPs like F* can help verify designs, they are not suitable for writing ICS software due to memory and type safety issues in embedded languages like C or C++. The paper leverages off-the-shelf RPC frameworks that use high-level message specifications to automatically generate boilerplate code, allowing engineers to focus on essential application logic.

Critically, the approach makes formally verifying all software in an ICS unnecessary. Instead, only software interfacing with physical mechanisms needs verification. For example, low-level software driving a servo or reading a thermometer. The proofs show that the ICS behaves safely if RPC semantics are accurately modeled; i.e., if the software implementation matches the F* semantics.

The paper demonstrates its approach using an example from the Fischertechnik Industry 4.0 platform and measures dynamic attestation’s impact on latency and throughput, showing moderate overhead but acceptable in many applications.
